data.title: Broadcom declares $0.59 dividend
data.description:
• Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) declared $0.59/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 0.91%
  • Payable June 30; for shareholders of record June 20; ex-div June 20.
  • See AVGO Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Superior Industries receives NYSE non-compliance notice
data.description:
• Superior Industries (NYSE:SUP) received a non-compliance notice from the NYSE as its 30-day average market cap was ~$45.9M, below the $50M minimum.
  • As of March 31, 2025, SUP reported a stockholders’ deficit of approximately $288.7M.
  • Superior has 45 days to submit a plan to regain compliance within 18 months, which will be subject to NYSE review.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Pan Global appoints CFO
data.description:
Pan Global Resources (OTCQX:PGZFF) (TSXV:PGZ:CA) announced on Thursday the appointment of Justin Byrd as CFO.

Byrd replaces Andy Marshall, who is set to remain with the company until June 30.

Byrd has served as CFO at Mayfair Gold until July 2024.

data.title: Rubrik Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.15 beats by $0.17, revenue of $278.5M beats by $18.09M
data.description:
• Rubrik press release (NYSE:RBRK): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.15 beats by $0.17.
  • Revenue of $278.5M (+48.7% Y/Y) beats by $18.09M.

  • Rubrik is providing the following guidance for the second quarter of fiscal year 2026 and the full fiscal year 2026:

    • Second Quarter Fiscal 2026 Outlook:
      • Revenue of $281 million to $283 million.
      • Non-GAAP Subscription ARR contribution margin of 4.5% to 5.5%.
      • Non-GAAP EPS of $(0.35) to $(0.33).
      • Weighted-average shares outstanding of approximately 196 million.
    • Full Year 2026 Outlook:
      • Subscription ARR between $1,380 million and $1,388 million.
      • Revenue of $1,179 million to $1,189 million.
      • Non-GAAP Subscription ARR contribution margin of approximately 6.0%.
      • Non-GAAP EPS of $(1.02) to $(0.96).
      • Weighted-average shares outstanding of approximately 198 million.
      • Free cash flow of $65 million to $75 million.\
      •

data.title: Mission Produce Non-GAAP EPS of $0.12 beats by $0.06, revenue of $380.3M beats by $84.15M
data.description:
• Mission Produce press release (NASDAQ:AVO): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.12 beats by $0.06.
  • Revenue of $380.3M (+27.8% Y/Y) beats by $84.15M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $19.1 million, compared to $20.2 million in the same period last year

data.title: Vail Resorts GAAP EPS of $10.54 beats by $0.48, revenue of $1.29B misses by $10M
data.description:
• Vail Resorts press release (NYSE:MTN): Q3 GAAP EPS of $10.54 beats by $0.48.
  • Revenue of $1.29B (+0.8% Y/Y) misses by $10M.
  • As a result of the lower than expected lift ticket visitation during the spring period announced on April 24, 2025, and one-time costs related to the CEO transition announced on May 27, 2025, the Company is updating its guidance for fiscal 2025. The Company now expects net income attributable to Vail Resorts, Inc. to be between $264 million and $298 million, and Resort Reported EBITDA for fiscal 2025 to be between $831 million and $851 million.

data.title: Tesla slides 14% after the Elon Musk-Donald Trump tiff goes off the rails
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) ended trading on Thursday with one of its worst declines in history after President Trump and Elon Musk engaged in an escalating war of words during the trading session.

An irritated Trump started off the day saying he was very disappointed with Elon Musk for criticizing his "big beautiful bill" and it ended with him floating the idea of terminating the government contracts of companies owned by Musk. In between, there were a shocking number of insults and grievances traded back and forth.

Investors sold Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) stock on concerns that the Trump premium may have faded and that there could be a new headwind for the electric vehicle maker, shortly before the crucial launch of its robotaxi service in the U.S. 

The 14.3% decline for Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) ranked as one of its top ten worst days ever on a percentage basis. On a year-to-date basis, shares of Tesla (TSLA) are down about 29%. The Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (TSLL) ended the session down close to 29%.

Elon Musk's other companies include SpaceX (SPACE), xAI, Neuralink, The Boring Company, Neuralink, and social media site X. Of note, private market trading on SpaceX (SPACE) indicated a decline of about 8% at last check. That share price slump may be linked to Trump's Truth Social post which stated, "The easiest way to save money in our Budget, Billions and Billions of Dollars, is to terminate Elon’s Governmental Subsidies and Contracts."

data.title: SL Green puts two prime NYC buildings on the market - report
data.description:
As demand for Manhattan office properties heat up, SL Green (NYSE:SLG), Manhattan's largest office landlord, reportedly has put up for sale a SoHo office building and a midtown property on Madison Avenue, according to a media report on Thursday.

The REIT hired Eastdil Secured to market 110 Greene Street in SoHo and 690 Madison Avenue, a building one block from Central Park, Bloomberg News reported, citing marketing materials that it viewed.

The two properties together are expected to get about $300M, a person familiar with the matter told Bloomberg.

Eastdil has helped broker ~$17B of capital markets assignments in New York City this year, encompassing deals that have recently closed or are currently active, the article said.

A representative for SLGreen (NYSE:SLG) didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

SL Green (SLG) stock rose 2.1% in trading during Thursday's regular session.

data.title: OGE Energy raised to Buy at Barclays on legislative boost
data.description:
OGE Energy (NYSE:OGE) +0.4% in Thursday's trading as Barclays upgraded shares to Overweight from Equal Weight with a $47 price target, up from $45, citing recent legislative developments and strong balance sheet as key positives for the utility.

Barlcays analyst Nicholas Campanella said the SB 998 legislation fundamentally changes OGE's (NYSE:OGE) ability to accelerate its capital spending program, with less frequent rate filings and improved cash recovery for large gas investments in the back half of the decade.

Despite trading at a 17% price-to-earnings premium to small-cap peers, Campanella said OGE's (OGE) premium is only ~7% compared to large-cap counterparts.

The analyst anticipates positive data points indicating an acceleration of regulated asset base growth, which could increase from the current 7.5%-9.5% based on announced and pending projects, as well as the potential for earnings per share growth, with every $1B of incremental capital spending potentially powering 400 MW of new load, translating to a 3%-4% boost to long-term EPS.

data.title: Brown-Forman outlines low single-digit organic net sales decline for fiscal 2026 amid volatile global spirits market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lawson E. Whiting reported that "Brown-Forman navigated the extremely challenging and uncertain operating environment by remaining focused on the long term, leveraging our strengths and executing our business strategies with a focus on improving our route to consumer in several markets, evolving our workforce to simplify and streamline our organization, allowing us to become more agile and efficient and growing our portfolio of brands through sponsorships, media campaigns and innovation." He highlighted that shipments closely matched depletions for the first time in six years, and after adjusting for divestitures and FX, organic net sales grew 1% for fiscal 2025, with a five-year organic net sales CAGR of 6%.
  • Woodford Reserve led organic net sales growth, with 8% organic net sales growth, and New Mix delivered another year of double-digit growth, surpassing 11 million 9-liter cases. CEO Whiting announced that New Mix will launch two flavors in key U.S. states this summer and shared the upcoming launch of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry later this summer, calling it a "globally recognized, well-established flavor trend."
  • Diplomatico achieved strong double-digit organic net sales growth, particularly in France, Germany, and travel retail. Gin Mare grew 1% organically, with a $47 million noncash impairment charge recognized for the brand name, and a $43 million reduction in contingent consideration liability, reflecting a more challenging macro environment in Europe.
  • CEO Whiting detailed workforce reductions and a cooperage closing, incurring $63 million in charges and forecasting $70 million to $80 million in annualized savings. He emphasized that "these strategic initiatives will ensure the company continues to endure for generations to come."
  • CFO Leanne D. Cunningham stated, "Our emerging international markets continue to lead our growth and collectively delivered a 9% organic net sales increase in fiscal 2025." She highlighted that strategic partnerships, geographic expansion, and innovation in RTDs and super premium brands are central to the company's growth.

Outlook

  • CFO Cunningham forecasted a "low single-digit decline in organic net sales" for fiscal 2026, citing a volatile operating environment with "sustained levels of consumer uncertainty" and the expectation that "behavior of the consumer and the level of trade inventories will not change meaningfully." She indicated that used barrel sales, a significant contributor in fiscal 2025, are expected to drop to "more than half of the fiscal 2025 level, making it a significant year-over-year headwind."
  • Organic operating income is also expected to "decline in the low single-digit range." The company anticipates capital expenditures between $125 million and $135 million and an effective tax rate of approximately 21% to 23%.

Financial Results

  • Brown-Forman reported a 5% decrease in reported net sales for fiscal 2025, while organic net sales grew 1%. Gross profit decreased 7%, with gross margin contracting 150 basis points to 58.9%.
  • Reported operating income decreased 22%, with organic operating income growing 3%. Diluted earnings per share decreased 14% to $1.84.
  • Cash of $350 million was received from the Duckhorn investment sale, resulting in a $78 million gain. Quarterly dividends paid totaled $420 million, and $300 million in long-term notes were repaid.

Q&A

  • Bryan Douglass Spillane, BofA Securities: Asked about the structural versus cyclical nature of consumer weakness in spirits. CEO Whiting responded that "the factors that are affecting the world really to me, still sound a whole lot more cyclical than they do structural," citing a global slowdown and persistent consumer wallet pressure.
  • Nadine Sarwat, Bernstein: Sought clarity on guidance assumptions and the impact of tariffs. CFO Cunningham explained that the outlook includes "the indirect impact from Canada, but then also the direct impact on some of our cost of goods," with disruption expected from "14 distributor transitions in key markets in the United States" and a significant step-down in used barrel sales. CEO Whiting reiterated the long-term mid-single-digit growth expectation but emphasized that "the environment continues to be really difficult."
  • Kevin Michael Grundy, BNP Paribas Exane: Inquired about pricing and industry supply. CEO Whiting stated, "low single digit on a regular basis is the pricing that we want to see out of Brown-Forman," and noted that industry pricing remains rational. CFO Cunningham highlighted continued investment in RTDs and innovation.
  • Lauren Rae Lieberman, Barclays: Asked about contingency plans if industry trends are more structural. CEO Whiting pointed to potential for "more pricing" and resource reallocation to emerging markets, emphasizing continued focus on premium brands.
  • Robert Bain Moskow, TD Cowen: Questioned Jack Daniel's international brand health and A&P spending. CEO Whiting said "we are seeing now meaningful improvement in the brand health measures" for Jack Daniel's and that no change in A&P philosophy is planned, with alignment to depletion-based expectations.
  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Sought details on developed international market weakness and the impact of the Korbel exit. CEO Whiting acknowledged European weakness and continued emerging market strength. CFO Cunningham clarified that Korbel's exclusion from reported results after June represents a $94 million top-line and $12 million operating income headwind.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism and concern about the persistence of weak spirits trends, structural changes in consumer behavior, guidance assumptions, and the company's ability to achieve its long-term growth algorithm. Questions frequently addressed consumer demand, pricing pressures, and the impact of tariffs, with a generally slightly negative to neutral tone.
  • Management maintained a realistic but cautiously optimistic tone in prepared remarks, shifting to a more defensive stance in Q&A. CEO Whiting frequently reiterated cyclical explanations for weakness and stressed ongoing strategic initiatives. CFO Cunningham provided detailed guidance assumptions and acknowledged volatility, using phrases such as "we believe," "we expect," and "we are working towards."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts were more explicit about the challenging outlook, with increased discussion of guidance headwinds, industry volatility, and the potential for structural change. Management's confidence appeared tempered by persistent external uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company moved from reaffirming its 2% to 4% organic net sales growth outlook for fiscal 2025 in the previous quarter to forecasting a low single-digit decline for fiscal 2026, citing macroeconomic volatility, tariff risk, and used barrel sales headwinds.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward cost control, workforce streamlining, and distributor changes in the U.S. Analysts continued to focus on the sustainability of spirits category growth, pricing, and the risk of structural shifts in consumer behavior.
  • Key metrics reflected increased pressure: reported net sales, gross profit, and EPS all declined year-over-year, while guidance language became more cautious. Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more defensive acknowledgement of persistent headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing volatility due to geopolitical uncertainties, global macroeconomic conditions, and tariff risk. Disruption is expected from distributor transitions and a substantial reduction in used barrel sales.
  • Analysts were concerned with consumer demand, pricing, inventory adjustments, and the potential for structural changes in the spirits market.
  • The company is closely monitoring tariffs and has built known impacts into guidance, but visibility remains low.

Final Takeaway

Brown-Forman management emphasized that while fiscal 2025 delivered positive organic growth amid an uncertain environment, expectations for fiscal 2026 are tempered by ongoing volatility, a low single-digit decline in organic net sales, and significant headwinds from used barrel sales and distributor transitions. The company remains focused on long-term brand building, innovation, and geographic expansion, while navigating short-term disruptions and maintaining a cautious but strategic approach to investment and cost management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
